title: Archived Results at a Glance – Evaluation of the Multi-Sectoral Partnerships - Canada.ca
content:
## Archived Results at a Glance – Evaluation of the Multi-Sectoral Partnerships
Office of Audit and Evaluation
The Healthy Living and Chronic Disease Prevention –  Multi-Sectoral Partnerships (MSP) Program was launched in 2013 by the Centre  for Chronic Disease Prevention and Health Equity (CCDPHE) in the Health  Promotion and Chronic Disease Prevention Branch. The Program co-invests in  projects to test and scale up the most promising primary prevention  interventions that address common modifiable risk factors for chronic disease.  Proposals are accepted on a continuous basis.
Funded projects featured an objective of changing behaviour  to reduce risk factors, beyond raising awareness; a strong theoretical or  evidence basis; a matched funding ratio of 1:1   from partners; “pay-for-performance” tied to measurable results, or  other social finance approaches in contribution agreements; and annual results  reporting, plus a project evaluation.
$101.6 M  - 2014-15 to 2018-19

## Evaluation  Approach
This evaluation assessed the relevance and performance of  the MSP Program from April 2014 to December 2018. Data collection activities  took place from August 2018 to March 2019. OAE also applied a SGBA+ lens to its  analysis, where appropriate.
- Document  and file review
- Key  informant interviews
- Financial  data review

### What the Evaluation Found
- The MSP Program  responds to an ongoing need to address common modifiable risk factors for  chronic disease.
- There are early  indications of success, in terms of number of individuals participating in  funded projects, knowledge development relating to healthy behaviours, and  evidence of progress towards behaviour change and improved health.
- At this early stage in  the Program's life cycle, it has not yet identified and shared what works or  does not work, for whom, and in what context, in terms of innovative  interventions and new models.
- This Program is  perceived as a leader among those within the Government of Canada who use  experimental program design and apply innovative funding models.
- The Program has  conducted Sex- and Gender-Based Analysis Plus (SGBA+) analyses in critical  areas, and is collecting information on populations targeted in individual  projects. That said, the Program has encountered challenges in applying SGBA+  due to its broad scope of chronic disease risk factors and current program  design.
- There are opportunities  to improve the Program's performance measurement practices, including setting  appropriate targets for each performance indicator and ensuring that data is  collected consistently at the project level.
- Considering the  complexities of its current design, the Program should determine how to  integrate SGBA+ findings into the design of projects, in order to attain the  strategic objectives of the Program.
- Revise current  performance measurement practices to ensure that performance data is being  collected consistently across all funded projects, to effectively measure  program impact, and to ensure that expected program-level results are being  appropriately tracked and communicated to guide future decision making on  project selection.
- Introduce a systematic  process to compile lessons learned on what is or isn't working, for whom, and  in what context, and share these lessons learned with internal and external  partners and stakeholders.
